verses.text: Hearing all this, Jezebel vows to kill Elijah, and the prophet who faced four hundred fifty alone now flees in terror for his life. The man mighty on Carmel collapses before a threat. Even the strongest saint, after great victory, can crumble in a moment of fear.

Hearing all this, Jezebel vows to kill Elijah, and the prophet who faced four hundred fifty alone now flees in terror for his life. The man mighty on Carmel collapses before a threat. Even the strongest saint, after great victory, can crumble in a moment of fear.

Hearing all this, Jezebel vows to kill Elijah, and the prophet who faced four hundred fifty alone now flees in terror for his life. The man mighty on Carmel collapses before a threat. Even the strongest saint, after great victory, can crumble in a moment of fear.

Alone in the wilderness, Elijah sits under a juniper and begs to die: 'It is enough; now, O LORD, take away my life.' Exhaustion and despair follow his great exertion. God's servants, spent and discouraged, may sink so low they wish for death itself.

But God does not rebuke him; an angel feeds him twice and lets him sleep, and in that strength he travels forty days to Horeb. Heaven's first answer to despair is gentle care — rest and food. God tends the body before He addresses the troubled soul.

But God does not rebuke him; an angel feeds him twice and lets him sleep, and in that strength he travels forty days to Horeb. Heaven's first answer to despair is gentle care — rest and food. God tends the body before He addresses the troubled soul.

But God does not rebuke him; an angel feeds him twice and lets him sleep, and in that strength he travels forty days to Horeb. Heaven's first answer to despair is gentle care — rest and food. God tends the body before He addresses the troubled soul.

But God does not rebuke him; an angel feeds him twice and lets him sleep, and in that strength he travels forty days to Horeb. Heaven's first answer to despair is gentle care — rest and food. God tends the body before He addresses the troubled soul.

At Horeb God asks, 'What doest thou here, Elijah?' and he pours out his complaint: he alone is left, and they seek his life. Self-pity has narrowed his view. God lets him speak his grief, even when that grief sees far less than the whole truth.

At Horeb God asks, 'What doest thou here, Elijah?' and he pours out his complaint: he alone is left, and they seek his life. Self-pity has narrowed his view. God lets him speak his grief, even when that grief sees far less than the whole truth.

Wind, earthquake, and fire pass, but the LORD is in none of them; then comes 'a still small voice.' God is not always in the dramatic. He who answered by fire on Carmel now meets His weary servant in a whisper, teaching that His gentlest word is still His.

Wind, earthquake, and fire pass, but the LORD is in none of them; then comes 'a still small voice.' God is not always in the dramatic. He who answered by fire on Carmel now meets His weary servant in a whisper, teaching that His gentlest word is still His.

Elijah repeats his complaint, and God answers with work to do — anoint Hazael, Jehu, Elisha — and a comfort: seven thousand have not bowed to Baal. He was never as alone as he felt. God cures despair with renewed purpose and the truth that His remnant remains.

Elijah repeats his complaint, and God answers with work to do — anoint Hazael, Jehu, Elisha — and a comfort: seven thousand have not bowed to Baal. He was never as alone as he felt. God cures despair with renewed purpose and the truth that His remnant remains.

Elijah repeats his complaint, and God answers with work to do — anoint Hazael, Jehu, Elisha — and a comfort: seven thousand have not bowed to Baal. He was never as alone as he felt. God cures despair with renewed purpose and the truth that His remnant remains.

Elijah repeats his complaint, and God answers with work to do — anoint Hazael, Jehu, Elisha — and a comfort: seven thousand have not bowed to Baal. He was never as alone as he felt. God cures despair with renewed purpose and the truth that His remnant remains.

Elijah finds Elisha plowing and casts his mantle on him; Elisha slays his oxen, burns his plow, and follows. The call comes, and he leaves all without looking back. To answer God's claim is to burn the bridges behind us and give ourselves wholly to His service.
